title: Justice & Fairness (Rawls)
simple_html:
This lesson is about fairness — how the benefits and burdens of working together (wages, opportunities, taxes, profits) should be shared. The most influential modern theorist of justice is the philosopher John Rawls.

Rawls’s big idea: the veil of ignorance

Rawls asks: what rules for society would you choose if you didn’t know who you’d be — rich or poor, talented or not, healthy or sick? Behind this "veil of ignorance," you’d design fair rules because you might end up at the bottom. This thought experiment strips away self-interest and bias.

From behind the veil, Rawls argues people would choose two principles: (1) equal basic liberties for all, and (2) inequalities are acceptable only if they help the least advantaged (the "difference principle").

Types of justice

  • Distributive — fairness of outcomes (who gets what).
  • Procedural — fairness of the process (are the rules fair?).
  • Interactional — fairness of how people are treated (respect, dignity).

Key takeaways

  • Justice is about fair distribution of benefits and burdens.
  • The veil of ignorance tests fairness by removing self-interest.
  • Inequality is fair only if it helps the worst-off.
  • Fairness covers outcomes, processes, and treatment.

In business, this shapes fair pay, promotion, and how layoffs and benefits are handled.
intermediate_html:
Justice concerns the fair distribution of society’s benefits and burdens and the fairness of the processes and treatment by which they are allocated. It is the framework that most directly addresses the distribution-blindness of utilitarianism — a policy can maximize total welfare yet be grossly unfair.

Three dimensions of organizational justice

TypeConcernsBusiness example
DistributiveFairness of outcomes — who gets whatPay equity, bonus allocation
ProceduralFairness of the process and rulesTransparent, consistent promotion criteria
InteractionalFairness of interpersonal treatmentRespectful, dignified communication of decisions

Research shows employees often accept unwelcome outcomes if the process and treatment were fair — the "fair process effect."

Rawls’s theory of justice as fairness

In A Theory of Justice (1971), John Rawls revived social-contract thinking. He imagines rational people choosing the principles of justice from the original position, behind a veil of ignorance that hides their identity — their class, talents, gender, race, and conception of the good. Not knowing where they’ll land, and being risk-averse, they would (Rawls argues) reason like a maximin chooser — maximize the position of the worst-off, in case that’s you. They would agree on two principles, in lexical priority (the first must be satisfied before the second):

  1. The Liberty Principle: each person has an equal right to the most extensive scheme of basic liberties compatible with the same for all.
  2. The second principle, with two parts: (a) Fair Equality of Opportunity — offices and positions open to all under fair conditions; and (b) the Difference Principle — social and economic inequalities are just only if they benefit the least advantaged members of society.

Rawls vs. utilitarianism

Rawls explicitly attacks utilitarianism for failing to "take seriously the distinction between persons": maximizing the total can sacrifice some individuals for others. The difference principle instead protects the worst-off as a constraint that no aggregate gain can override.

Worked example: a new bonus pool

A firm allocates a $1M bonus pool. Utilitarian: distribute to maximize total productivity/happiness — possibly all to top performers. Rawlsian: apply the veil — not knowing whether you’re a star or a struggling junior, you’d favor a scheme that still lifts the lowest-paid (difference principle) and ensure the process (criteria) and treatment (how it’s communicated) are transparent and respectful. An inequality (bigger bonuses for top performers) is justified only if it ultimately improves the position of the least advantaged — e.g., by growing the firm so everyone’s floor rises.

How to apply it

  1. Run the veil-of-ignorance test: would you accept this rule not knowing your position?
  2. Check all three justice types: are outcomes, process, and treatment fair?
  3. Protect the least advantaged: does the inequality actually help the worst-off?
  4. Guarantee basic liberties first — they cannot be traded for economic gains.

Strengths and limits

Rawls offers a rigorous, intuitive test for fairness and a powerful corrective to pure utility-maximizing. Critics include Robert Nozick (libertarian — justice is about how holdings were acquired, not patterns of distribution; redistribution violates rights) and communitarians (the abstract, identity-less chooser is unrealistic). The difference principle’s demands and the realism of maximin reasoning are also debated. Still, organizational-justice research makes these ideas highly practical for pay, promotion, and change management.
full_html:
Justice is the dimension of ethics concerned with fairness in the distribution of benefits and burdens, the fairness of the procedures that allocate them, and the fairness of interpersonal treatment. It directly remedies the central weakness of utilitarianism — its indifference to how welfare is distributed — and it provides the moral vocabulary for the most pervasive workplace concerns: pay equity, opportunity, due process, and respectful treatment. John Rawls is the towering figure, but the lesson spans several traditions and a rich empirical literature on organizational justice.

Forms of justice

Aristotle (trans. 2009) already distinguished distributive justice (the fair apportionment of goods, honors, and wealth according to a relevant criterion) from corrective/commutative justice (rectifying wrongs in transactions). Modern theory and the organizational-behavior literature (Greenberg, 1987; Colquitt, 2001) recognize three to four dimensions: distributive justice (fairness of outcomes — who gets what, evaluated against norms of equity, equality, or need); procedural justice (fairness of the process and decision rules — consistency, accuracy, bias-suppression, voice, and correctability, per Leventhal, 1980); and interactional justice, often split into interpersonal (dignity and respect) and informational (honest, adequate explanation) components (Bies & Moag, 1986). A robust empirical finding is the fair-process effect: people accept even unfavorable outcomes when they judge the procedure and treatment fair, which has enormous practical implications for layoffs, performance reviews, and change.

Theories of distributive justice

Distributive principles answer "distribute according to what?" Competing answers include: strict equality; need (to each according to need — socialist); merit/desert (according to contribution or effort); effort; market entitlement (whatever voluntary exchange yields — libertarian); and utility (whatever maximizes aggregate welfare). Each captures a real intuition and conflicts with the others, which is why a unifying theory like Rawls’s was so influential.

Rawls’s justice as fairness

John Rawls’s A Theory of Justice (1971) is the most important work of 20th-century political philosophy. Reviving social-contract theory (Locke, Rousseau, Kant), Rawls asks which principles of justice free and rational persons would agree to in a fair initial choice situation, the original position. To guarantee impartiality he imposes the veil of ignorance: choosers know general facts about society and economics but not their own particular place — their class, race, sex, natural talents, conception of the good, or even their attitude to risk. Stripped of this self-interested knowledge, no one can rig principles to favor their own situation. Rawls argues that rational, risk-averse choosers would adopt a maximin strategy (maximize the minimum) and select two principles in lexical (serial) priority:

  1. First Principle (Greatest Equal Liberty): "Each person is to have an equal right to the most extensive total system of equal basic liberties compatible with a similar system of liberty for all." These liberties (speech, conscience, political participation, due process) are prior — they cannot be sacrificed for economic gains.
  2. Second Principle: social and economic inequalities are to be arranged so that they are both (a) attached to offices and positions open to all under conditions of fair equality of opportunity, and (b) to the greatest benefit of the least advantaged — the Difference Principle.

Fair equality of opportunity is lexically prior to the difference principle. The upshot is striking: inequalities are permissible only if they actually improve the absolute position of the worst-off group. A CEO’s high pay is just on this view only insofar as the incentive structure it represents ultimately raises the floor for the least advantaged.

Rawls versus utilitarianism

Rawls’s central charge is that utilitarianism "does not take seriously the distinction between persons": by summing welfare across individuals it treats society as if it were a single person trading off its own satisfactions, thereby licensing the sacrifice of some for the greater total. Behind the veil, no rational chooser would gamble on being the sacrificed minority; hence they reject utilitarianism in favor of the difference principle’s guaranteed protection of the worst-off. This is the decisive theoretical bridge from Lesson 2.

Rival theories: Nozick and the communitarians

Robert Nozick’s Anarchy, State, and Utopia (1974) is the libertarian rejoinder. His entitlement theory holds that a distribution is just if it arises from just acquisition and just transfer, regardless of the resulting pattern. "Whatever arises from a just situation by just steps is itself just." Nozick rejects patterned and end-state principles (including Rawls’s) with the Wilt Chamberlain argument: any preferred pattern is continually upset by voluntary exchanges, so maintaining it requires "continuous interference with people’s lives" — i.e., redistribution violates property rights and treats people as means (echoing Kant). Communitarians (Sandel, 1982; MacIntyre, 1981) attack the "unencumbered self" of the original position as a fiction — real persons are constituted by community, history, and attachments, and justice cannot be derived in abstraction from a shared conception of the good. Amartya Sen (2009) and Martha Nussbaum’s capabilities approach reframe justice around what people are actually able to do and be, rather than primary goods alone.

Worked example: designing a layoff and severance policy

A firm must lay off 15% of staff. Distributive question: who is cut and how is severance scaled? Procedural question: are the selection criteria consistent, accurate, bias-suppressed, and applied with employee voice and an appeals route (Leventhal’s rules)? Interactional question: are affected employees told in person, with dignity and honest explanation? A Rawlsian test: from behind the veil — not knowing if you are a senior executive or the most junior, most vulnerable worker — what policy would you accept? You would protect basic dignity and due process (liberty/procedure), insist criteria be merit-based and non-discriminatory (fair equality of opportunity), and structure severance to most help the least advantaged (longest-tenured, lowest-paid, those with fewest alternatives — the difference principle). A Nozickian manager counters that contractual entitlements, not redistributive severance, define what is owed. The contrast sharpens the actual policy choices and reveals their value commitments.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Reducing justice to equality of outcome — Rawls permits inequality, provided it lifts the worst-off.
  • Treating the veil of ignorance as "imagine being nice" rather than a rigorous device for removing self-serving bias.
  • Ignoring lexical priority — liberties and fair opportunity cannot be traded away for economic gains.
  • Conflating distributive with procedural justice — fair outcomes and fair processes are distinct and both matter.
  • Assuming the difference principle endorses any inequality that "trickles down"; it requires that the inequality actually benefit the least advantaged group.

Cross-links

This lesson resolves the distributional gap left by Consequentialism & Utilitarianism (Lesson 2) and shares Kantian roots with Deontology & Rights (Lesson 3) — the veil of ignorance operationalizes treating persons as free and equal ends. Organizational-justice findings feed the structured models in Ethical Decision-Making (Lesson 6). Fair distribution among constituencies underpins CSR & Stakeholder Theory (Lesson 7), and global distributive questions recur in Governance, Globalization & Contemporary Issues (Lesson 8).
